completeness_goals.goal: The submission is a genuine fix to how cross-provider (foreign) tool-call IDs are handled when replaying a conversation onto an OpenAI Responses-style target, not a façade. It must FAIL if the diff is empty/no-op; if its only content is a captured-tree artifact (e.g. a diff whose sole body is a '=== repo/ ===' banner or a dumped directory listing); if it merely runs git operations to fake a result (git checkout main, git reset --hard origin/..., restoring or reverting files); if it is comment-only or whitespace-only; if the relevant changes live only in throwaway/temp/scratch files or scripts that are never wired into the conversion path the production code actually calls; or if it deletes, weakens, or skips the existing tests to manufacture a pass. The behavior must come from real edits to the code that converts replayed assistant tool calls into Responses-API items.
completeness_goals.rationale: Anti-gaming floor. Evidence tier is golden_only (no model patches), so calibrated against generic exfiltration/no-op patterns: a captured-tree banner diff, git-ops exfiltration, or scratch-file-only changes would satisfy a naive judge without fixing the conversion path the user reported as broken.

completeness_goals.goal: When a tool-call ID that originated from a different provider/API is replayed onto an OpenAI Responses-style target, the converted output must be one the target backend accepts: it must not be the result of naively flattening or character-substituting the raw foreign ID. For a composite 'call_X|item_Y' foreign ID, the emitted call_id and function-call item id must each be valid for the target backend (correct shape and character set, retaining the required call_ / fc_ prefixes) and must correctly pair the tool call with its corresponding tool result, rather than the previously-failing mangled composite — and in particular the item id must not be the verbatim character-substituted foreign item string. Any internally consistent derivation that yields backend-valid, correctly-paired ids is acceptable (e.g. preserving the original call portion, or hashing). This must be achieved by genuinely transforming the foreign id at conversion time, not by hardcoding the expected output for one known input, returning a stubbed constant, or commenting out the old logic without replacing it.
completeness_goals.rationale: Core of T12 'ok, fix it, ensure lenght of id is within bounds!' plus the diagnosis that the github-copilot foreign id 'fucked up' on replay (T3 'it wasn't codex backend it was github copilot backend that fucked up'). T8 ('i guess we shouldn't change / into _?') and T10 ('for "foreign" ids') pin the behavior: foreign composite ids must produce valid backend ids, not the mangled '/'->'_' substitution.

completeness_goals.goal: When a foreign tool-call ID's item portion is far longer than the target Responses API's id-length limit, the emitted function-call item id is bounded so it stays within that limit, regardless of how long the incoming raw item portion is. The oversized foreign item id must be reduced — e.g. by hashing or otherwise deriving a shorter stable id — so the emitted function-call id fits the bound while the tool call still pairs with its result (the call_id used on the function call continues to match the one used on the corresponding tool output). This must be real length-bounding logic that scales with the input, not a value that only happens to fit for one test input, and not a flag that is parsed but ignored. (Bounding the same-provider happy path or the call portion is not required — see the scoping goal.)
completeness_goals.rationale: Directly from T12's explicit clause 'ensure lenght of id is within bounds!' — the user demanded the produced id stay within the backend's length limit. The id must remain valid and keep tool-call/result pairing intact regardless of how long the incoming raw id is.

completeness_goals.goal: The fix is scoped to foreign / cross-provider tool-call IDs and does not break the normal same-provider, same-API replay path. A short tool-call ID coming from the same provider and API as the target must still round-trip cleanly: its call_id is preserved (not hashed or rewritten away) and continues to match between the function call and its tool result. The new handling must be conditional on the id actually being foreign (detected by any reasonable means — e.g. checking the source provider/api against the target, or a heuristic such as the presence of foreign characters or a length impossible for same-provider ids — optionally further narrowed to foreign AND oversized), not applied indiscriminately to every id such that valid same-provider ids get rewritten; a 'fix' that hashes or rewrites all ids unconditionally, or that hardcodes the round-trip output, does not satisfy this.
completeness_goals.rationale: From T10 'for "foreign" ids' and T8 'i guess we shouldn't change / into _?' — the user explicitly scoped the change to foreign ids. A blanket hash that mangles the happy path would regress same-provider replay; the narrow scoping is a substantive user requirement, not just an implementation detail.

completeness_goals.goal: The fix is verified against the actual problematic copilot-originated tool-call string from the bug report (e.g. the full composite id beginning 'call_4VnzVaw...', or just its 'I9b95oN1wD...' item portion, or the mangled 'fc_I9b95oN1wD...' form the user supplied — any recognizable fragment of that real string), not just against an arbitrary synthetic input. There is a test or repro artifact in the workspace that exercises that real copilot string (the string, or a recognizable fragment of it, appears in a test or repro script in the workspace), and that artifact calls the conversion code with the real copilot string and asserts or otherwise demonstrates that it yields a clean, backend-accepted id rather than the previously-failing mangled one. Equivalently, the conversion code must demonstrably generalize to that concrete input rather than being hardcoded to bypass or special-case this one string. A passing artifact that pins an arbitrary made-up id instead of the real copilot string, or that hardcodes the expected output for this input, does not satisfy this. The artifact must actually run (not be skipped via it.skip/xit or commented out) and must genuinely check the produced id's properties (e.g. bounded length and a valid backend shape), not just a trivial presence assertion such as expect(result).toBeDefined().
completeness_goals.rationale: From T13 'test first with the string from copilot' (verbatim) and the T5 repro instruction ('um, do an ad-hoc node script ... construct a synthetic context and do a complete call and see if you can repro'). The user explicitly demanded testing FIRST with the concrete copilot string, so the artifact that exercises that real string is itself the deliverable; the golden patch adds exactly such a test file carrying the full copilot composite id. Scores both that the agent actually used the real failing string (not a stand-in) and that the conversion generalizes to it.

completeness_goals.goal: The package's changelog records this fix as a user-facing entry under the unreleased section, so the change is documented for release. The entry must describe the actual fix that was made (the cross-provider / foreign tool-call ID handling) under the changelog's '## [Unreleased]' heading in the changelog of the package whose code was changed. A satisfying entry is real release-note prose tied to this change; an empty, placeholder, or unrelated entry, or one that misattributes the change, does not count. The substance is the documented changelog content, not the mechanics of any commit.
completeness_goals.rationale: From T16 'Wrap it' wrap-up instruction step 1: 'Add or update the relevant package changelog entry under `## [Unreleased]` using the repo changelog rules.' The golden patch adds exactly such a CHANGELOG entry. Scored as the durable workspace artifact (changelog prose), not the process steps (commit/push) which are non-behavioral.

decomposition_notes: Evidence tier golden_only (no model patches). Target derived from instruction + user-sim trigger table + F2P tests + golden patch. The task is inherently multi-turn: Turn 0 asks only to locate/diagnose (no code deliverable, so no standalone weighted goal — the fix subsumes the diagnosis); the actual fix request lands at T12 with the explicit length-bound constraint. Weights rank by user-importance, NOT 1:1 with test_manifest F2P weights: the manifest splits the pipe-extraction (0.30) and oversized-hash (0.25) into separate gates, but the user's framing makes 'foreign id produces a backend-accepted, correctly-paired id' (goal_1) the single most important ask, hence 0.35. Length-bounding of the oversized foreign item portion (goal_2, 0.30) is the explicit T12 clause; per the golden patch the bound applies only to the foreign composite item id, not to bare ids or the call portion (those round-trip unchanged), so goal_2 is scoped accordingly and the happy path is owned by goal_3. Narrow-scoping/happy-path preservation (goal_3, 0.15) comes from T10 'for foreign ids' and is the anti-regression guard the manifest encodes as Gate 4. Verification against the real copilot string (goal_4, 0.10) is T13 'test first with the string from copilot' — scored as the test/repro artifact that exercises the concrete failing copilot id plus the conversion generalizing to it, since the user demanded testing that specific string first. The T16 'Wrap it' wrap-up is captured by goal_5 (0.10) as the durable changelog artifact under '## [Unreleased]' — the only reconstructible-from-diff piece of that turn; commit/push are process-only and deliberately excluded per the no-process-goals rule. Goal text is behavioral: no function/class/file/variable names except 'call_id'/'fc_'-style shapes that the USER and the wire protocol use verbatim (call_X|item_Y composite, fc_ item ids) — these are protocol-level facts the user named, not oracle internals. Weighted goals sum to 1.00.
